The NYI is most vulnerable to hypothermia during the first few hours after birth, although the condition may occur later too, for example during bathing, on a cold night, or during transportation, if measures to keep the baby warm are inadequate. Sick or low birth weight babies admitted to neonatal units with hypothermia are more likely to die than those admitted with normal temperatures.

Axillary temperature: This method is the routine practice for measuring temperature. It has fewer risks than rectal measurements in terms of injury or infection. Point the tip of the thermometer to the shoulder not the bed to avoid the thermometer reading the air temperature. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.

Rectal temperature: Do not use this method for routine monitoring. However, it is the best guide for core temperature in cold (hypothermic) sick neonates. Lubricate the thermometer, insert the tip of the thermometer into the rectum not more than an inch. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.
section: Introduction > Temperature Recording Using a Digital Thermometer

text: An easy way to assess a newborn baby’s temperature is by “touch”. This can be easily taught to caregivers. The baby’s abdomen is felt with the back of hand and compared with their own forehead. Abdominal temperature represents the core temperature and it is reliable in the diagnosis of hypothermia. Warm and pink feet of the baby indicate that the baby is in thermal comfort. But when feet are cold and trunk is warm, it indicates that the baby has cold stress.
section: Introduction > Assessment of Temperature by Touch

text:
The NYI has a normal body temperature between 36.5‑37.4 °C.

**Classification table**
- **Mild**: 36.0‑36.4 °C
- **Moderate**: 32.0‑35.9 °C
- **Severe**: < 32 °C

A community‑based study has found that mortality increased by approximately 80 % for every degree Celsius decrease in first observed axillary temperature, and that relative risk of death ranged from 2 to 30 times for moderate hypothermia, increasing with greater severity. Other epidemiological data from India, Malawi and additional studies are summarized in the source text.
Newborn baby’s temperature falls within seconds of being born and sick NYI are often hypothermic. If the temperature continues to fall the baby will become sick and may even die.
section: Classification of Hypothermia > Four ways a neonate may lose heat to the environment

text: These are procedures to be taken at birth and during the next few hours and days in order to minimize heat loss in all newborns.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn

text: If there are no signs of distress, a mother can provide a warm environment with skin‑to‑skin contact for the baby. If the baby is < 2500 g this should be continued as KMC. Place the baby, with a nappy and hat, upright inside mother’s clothing against mother’s bare skin over the chest. A loose blouse, sweater, or wrap tied at the waist holds the baby. The baby should wear a hat. Let baby suckle at the breast as often as needed, but at least every 2 hours.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Skin‑to‑skin Contact (Kangaroo Mother Care)

text: Bathing should be delayed until at least 24 hours after birth. Blood, meconium and some vernix will have been wiped off during drying at birth. The remaining vernix does not need to be removed; it may reduce heat loss and is re‑absorbed through the skin during the first days of life.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Postpone Bathing

text: Weighing the baby at birth puts him/her at risk of heat loss and should be postponed for at least two hours unless the room temperature is warm.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Postpone Weighing

text: As a rule, newborns need one or two more layers of clothing and bedding than adults. Covers should not be tight to allow air spaces between the layers as trapped air is a very efficient insulator. Keep ambient atmospheric temperature warm for the baby’s weight and post‑natal age. Monitor body temperature every 4 hours.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Appropriate Clothing and Bedding

text: If a baby cannot stay with their mother for KMC, a hot‑cot is helpful. The Blantyre hot‑cot is a simple incubator that uses four 60‑watt light bulbs to raise the air temperature within the cot by 1.5 °C per bulb. A baby may need one, two, three or all four bulbs on to stay warm. Check the baby’s temperature after an hour in the cot; if temperature increases, turn off two bulbs to avoid overheating and re‑check regularly.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Place in a hot‑cot (warmer) when mother cannot stay with the baby

text: The NYI should be re‑warmed carefully and gradually to prevent seizures; the temperature needs to rise by 0.5 °C per hour. The method selected depends on how sick the NYI is and on the availability of mother, staff and equipment.
section: Treatment of Established Hypothermia in the NYI

text:
Radiant warmers may be used on any neonatal patient admitted to the nursery ward, but especially for:

- Initial assessment of a sick or premature baby
- Hypothermia
- Undertaking invasive procedures
- Resuscitation
- When stabilising a sick baby

See the dedicated Radiant Warmer module for more information.

Hypoglycaemia is common in low‑birth‑weight and very sick newborn infants (NYI) and should always be considered early in management. 20 % of infants below seven days of age have hypoglycaemia. It is important to identify hypoglycaemia because it is associated with convulsions, permanent brain injury and increased mortality.

Begin when any visible jaundice is present at birth, jaundice extends below the umbilicus, or bilirubin reaches treatment thresholds. Maintain baby naked except for a nappy, cover eyes, turn baby every 2 h, monitor bilirubin daily, ensure adequate feeding, and watch for dehydration.
Indicated when bilirubin rises above specific cut‑off levels (see Phototherapy chapter for exact values).

The letters C in “ABCCCD” stand for Circulation, Coma and Convulsions. This module will help with the systematic assessment, resuscitation and treatment of all NYI with life‑threatening conditions that are most frequently seen in infants less than 2 months of age.

Shock, also called ‘severe impaired circulation’, can be defined as the life‑threatening failure of adequate oxygen delivery to the tissues and may be due to decreased blood perfusion of tissues, inadequate blood oxygen saturation, or increased oxygen demand from the tissues that results in decreased end‑organ oxygenation and dysfunction. If left untreated, shock results in sustained multiple organ dysfunction, end‑organ damage and possible death.

All sick infants are assessed for Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Coma, Convulsions and severe Dehydration (ABCCCD). Efforts should be made to maintain normal blood glucose and a normal body temperature while managing ABCCCD.

Rapid assessment of circulation after airway and breathing. Signs include: baby alert?, hands cold?, temperature gradient up limbs?, capillary refill time (CRT) > 3 s?, pulse fast and weak?. Shock is present when 3 or more signs are noted.
section: Assessment > Assess Circulation for Signs of Shock

text: Look for dehydration (sunken eyes/decreased skin turgor), pallor, severe wasting/oedema, and assess oxygen saturation, heart rate and blood pressure.

Capillary refill is assessed by applying finger pressure to the centre of the chest over the sternum for 3 seconds. The time from release of pressure to full return of pink colour should be < 3 seconds. Longer times suggest shock, unless the environment is cold.
A strong, non‑fast radial pulse (≤ 160 bpm) is adequate. Absence of a radial pulse or a weak/faster pulse requires checking the femoral pulse and assessing hydration status.
Treatment of shock requires teamwork. The following actions need to be started simultaneously, beginning with fluid administration for shock or impaired circulation.

NYI have immature immune systems and have just been colonised with bacteria during their recent delivery. They are therefore prone to infections which are likely to cross barriers (lungs‑blood, gut‑blood, meninges). Many NYI infections can be prevented by good hygiene at birth, early and exclusive breastfeeding, appropriate umbilical cord care, appropriate eye care, using Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) and avoiding separation of the mother and infant.

Common systemic bacterial infections in young infants include sepsis, pneumonia, urinary tract infection and meningitis and all these may present alike. Sepsis is a clinical syndrome of systemic illness accompanied by septicaemia (bacteria in the blood). It is also called bacteraemia.

**Risk Factors for Sepsis and Clues to Infection**

- Intrapartum maternal fever (temperature > 38 °C)
- Membranes ruptured > 18 h before delivery
- Foul‑smelling or purulent amniotic fluid
- Untreated STI such as syphilis
- Suspected or confirmed sepsis in a twin

Babies born to mothers with these risk factors may be symptomatic or asymptomatic. Both groups should be treated as having sepsis with IV antibiotics because the risk of neonatal infection and mortality rates are high.

ABCCCD – Assess ABCD and manage the problems identified. Provide supportive care and monitoring for the sick NYI.

Start empiric antibiotics; give penicillin and gentamicin – see doses in wall‑charts. Give (flu)cloxacillin instead of penicillin if extensive skin pustules or abscesses are present as these may indicate a staphylococcal infection.

It is a way of communicating with our colleagues, remembering important events and monitoring progress. Good documentation prevents errors and protects both the patient and the staff.
It is essential that the mother and baby are treated as one unit. The mother needs to know what is happening to her baby and how she can help. We must make her feel welcome, important and central to the well‑being of her baby.
The health workers are a team. We need to communicate with each other, respect and value each other and include the mother in the care of her baby. She is an extended part of the team.
The death of a baby is devastating to the mother and the baby’s family. We must be sensitive to their concern and grief, respectful of religious and cultural beliefs and be prepared to express our sorrow at their loss. Medical diagnoses and treatment given need to be explained to the family. It is important for the family to know that their loss is through no fault of the mother and she should not be blamed.

Introduction of essential devices to newborn care units is critical to improving newborn survival.  Devices can increase hospital‑acquired infections if adequate disinfection and cleaning measures are not put into place.  This module focuses on infection‑control measures associated specifically with essential devices, staff and visitors.  For comprehensive guidance see local and WHO guidelines.

Hospitals, wards, equipment and staff are all sources of infection for a baby.  Hospital‑acquired infections (HAI) are often caused by multidrug‑resistant bacteria.  Prevention strategies include hand hygiene, environmental cleanliness, medical‑equipment maintenance and waste disposal.  Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) are key elements.

Hand hygiene is the single most important measure to reduce transmission of infections.  All HCWs, guardians and visitors should wash hands and forearms up to the elbow with soap and water on entry, dry with single‑use towels, and re‑wash whenever visibly soiled.  Alcohol‑based hand‑rub is acceptable when hands are not visibly dirty.  Hand hygiene should be the last action before patient contact and the first action after.

Gloves protect both HCWs and patients but do not replace hand hygiene.  Wear gloves only when necessary (e.g., contact with bodily fluids, invasive procedures, contaminated surfaces) and discard immediately after use.  Perform hand washing or sanitising promptly after glove removal.

Staff should wear clean clothing with bare arms below the elbow.  No jewellery (except plain wedding bands) and nails must be short, natural and unvarnished.  Gowns, when used, must not be shared or reused until re‑washed.
Any HCW or guardian with an acute or transmissible infection must not be on the ward.  Guardians with acute respiratory illness should wear a mask and practise meticulous hand washing.

Isolation policies should cohort at‑risk patients with similar infections.  Strict hand hygiene is required on entry and exit.  Equipment used in isolation areas must be cleaned and disinfected before returning to the main ward.

All neonatal equipment (suctions, CPAP, monitors, warmers, etc.) must be cleaned according to manufacturer instructions.  WHO recommends 0.5 % sodium hypochlorite for surfaces contaminated with blood/body fluids; 70 % alcohol for metal and rubber surfaces.

All horizontal surfaces (bed rails, tables, trolleys, commodes, taps, scales, etc.) must be cleaned and disinfected with an approved detergent or 0.5 % chlorine/70 % alcohol solution at least daily and whenever visibly soiled.  Between patient admissions, cots, incubators and patient beds must be cleaned thoroughly.  Floors, handles and other surfaces are cleaned daily per departmental policy.

Do NOT recap used needles, do NOT remove needles from syringes by hand, and do NOT bend or break used needles.  Place syringes, lancets, needles and other sharps promptly in puncture‑resistant containers.

All single‑use devices should be used once, but in resource‑limited settings they are often reused.  Departmental policy must be strictly followed; cleaning must occur promptly after use.  Respiratory circuits, tubing and equipment are commonly reprocessed, whereas syringes, needles and disposable gloves must never be reprocessed.

chapter: Routine Care of the Normal Newborn Infant (Chapter 2)

text:
The vast majority of newborns require no intervention at birth other than routine normal care.  If this is done well, it vastly reduces the likelihood of problems.  Most newborns should be transferred to the post‑natal wards for rooming‑in with their mothers.  These babies still need to be monitored because they are at continued risk of hypothermia and feeding difficulties during the first few days of life.  They can also become sick and develop danger signs.  The mother needs counselling and appropriate treatment when required.  Babies born in health facilities should not be sent home in the crucial first 48 hours of life.  Follow‑up care should be organised before the mothers are discharged (see the chapter on discharge from the hospital).  Take time to counsel her, involve another guardian where possible, ensure they understand the implications of leaving early, advise them on danger signs and arrange a follow‑up plan for the baby.

A post‑natal room should be kept warm with no draughts from open doors or windows.  A room temperature of at least 25 °C is required to help keep the baby warm.  A mother and her baby should be kept together from birth if possible – this promotes bonding and allows the mother to respond quickly when the baby wants to feed, which helps establish and sustain breastfeeding.

"Is the baby sucking well?" – discuss breastfeeding progress within the first two days after delivery.
"Has the baby passed stools?" – term babies should pass meconium by 24 h; later is abnormal.
"Has the baby passed urine?" – urine should be passed by 48 h; later is abnormal.
"Is the baby jaundiced (yellow)?" – jaundice within the first 24 h should be evaluated.

Benefits of Breastfeeding – exclusive breastfeeding for six months gives the greatest health benefits and disease prevention.  Colostrum (the first yellowish milk) meets the newborn’s needs in the first few days.  Short‑term breast‑feed discomfort for the mother is common but should not persist.

Benefits to the mother include decreased risk of breast, uterine and ovarian cancers; natural contraception (LAM) for the first six months; and reduction of postpartum haemorrhage.

text: This is the critical period of transition from intra‑utero life to extra‑utero independent existence. Effective care at birth is needed to anticipate problems with this transition and to provide support to ensure stabilization. Most babies born with apnoea at birth will start to breathe themselves within 60–90 seconds if they have a clear airway.
section: Why is care at birth important?

text: Approximately 10 % of newborns require some assistance to begin breathing at birth; very few, only about 1 % need more than basic resuscitation to survive.

These are deliveries where it is more likely that resuscitation will be required. They include deliveries to mothers who are sick, who had a previous foetal or neonatal death, with pre‑eclampsia or eclampsia, with multiple pregnancies, or who are delivering preterm. Delivery circumstances that increase the risk are abnormal presentation, prolonged second stage, prolapsed cord, prolonged rupture of membranes, and meconium staining of the liquor.
For many infants, resuscitation cannot be anticipated before delivery. Therefore be prepared for resuscitation at **every** delivery.

The recommended temperature for the delivery room is 25 °C. Shut windows, turn off AC/fans, warm the resuscitaire 30 min before delivery and have at least two pre‑warmed absorbent towels – one to dry the baby and one to wrap the baby.
Nurse/midwife should identify a helper, explain roles and allocate duties according to skill level (qualified staff, untrained staff, or relative).
Check that all equipment and supplies are available, clean and functional before birth. Equipment should be positioned for immediate use.

Most newborns require only simple supportive care at and after delivery. Deliver the baby onto the mother’s abdomen, note the time of birth and wait one minute before clamping the pulsating cord. Use two dry warm cloths – one to dry and a fresh one to wrap. Immediate drying prevents heat loss and often stimulates breathing.
Assessment‑Decision table (crying → routine care; not crying but chest rising → routine care; gasping, not breathing, or very poor tone → start resuscitation immediately).
section: Baby is Delivered > How do you determine whether the baby requires resuscitation?

text: 1. Dry the infant with a clean cloth and observe. 2. Maintain skin‑to‑skin contact. 3. Cover to prevent heat loss. 4. Clamp and cut cord after ≥1 min. 5. Encourage breast‑feeding within the first hour. 6. Skin‑to‑skin & early breast‑feeding keep infant warm and prevent hypoglycaemia.
section: Baby is Delivered > Key Facts for Providers – How to Provide Essential Newborn Care at Delivery

text: If the baby requires resuscitation provide warmth with a radiant warmer or a warming cot. Position yourself at the bedside (head of the baby) so the chest and abdomen remain unobstructed.
A – Ensure Airway is open and clear. B – Ensure Breathing (spontaneous or assisted). C – Ensure adequate Circulation of oxygenated blood. Maintain body temperature throughout.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > ABCs of Resuscitation

text: Open airway by slightly extending the neck, maintain neutral position, use a rolled cloth if occiput is large. Clear secretions by wiping or suctioning (mouth‑first, then nose) with a Penguin suction device or catheter.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > A. Airway

text: Indications: baby not breathing or gasping, HR <100 bpm despite spontaneous breathing, persistent central cyanosis. Use appropriately sized masks (round or anatomically shaped). Position mask covering nose, mouth and chin; hold with thumb‑index‑middle fingers and lift the chin with ring/fifth fingers. Bag‑mask has a pop‑off valve set at ~30‑40 cm H₂O to prevent excessive pressure. Deliver 40‑60 breaths/min (≈1 breath/sec). For term infants use room air; for preterm ≤32 weeks start with 30 % O₂ (or air if blender unavailable). Assess effectiveness by chest rise, heart‑rate >100 bpm, improved colour and spontaneous effort.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > B. Positive‑Pressure Ventilation (Bag‑Mask)

text: Begin compressions if HR <60 bpm after 30 seconds of effective ventilation. Two providers are needed – one for ventilation (head of the baby) and one for compressions (side or foot). Techniques: Thumb technique (thumbs on sternum, fingers under back) or 2‑finger technique (middle finger + index/ring finger). Position hands on the lower third of the sternum (halfway between xiphoid and line between nipples). Compress to a depth of ~1⁄3 AP chest diameter, release fully. Ratio 3 compressions : 1 ventilation (3:1) ≈ 90 compressions/min. After ~30 seconds reassess HR. If HR >60 bpm stop compressions, continue ventilation at 40‑60 breaths/min. If HR remains <60 bpm continue CPR until HR >100 bpm or the baby is deemed non‑viable.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > C. Circulation – Chest Compressions

text: Discontinue efforts when: 1) infant is not breathing and no detectable heartbeat after 10 min; or 2) no spontaneous breathing and HR < 60 bpm after 20 min of effective resuscitation. Record the event, inform the parents and allow them to hold the infant if they wish.
A visual flow‑chart summarising the steps from initial assessment through airway, breathing, chest compressions and post‑resuscitation care.

Why are breathing difficulties in the newborn so important? Breathing difficulties are the most common way that sick neonates present to the healthcare worker. There are several different possible diagnoses and these need to be considered in order to provide the correct management.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)

text: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) occurs primarily in premature infants; its incidence is inversely related to gestational age and birthweight. It occurs in 60–80 % of infants < 28 weeks, 15–30 % of those 32‑36 weeks, about 5 % beyond 37 weeks and rarely at term. Surfactant deficiency is the primary cause. Risk is increased by maternal diabetes, multiple births, Caesarean section, precipitous delivery, asphyxia, cold stress and a history of previously affected infants; reduced by antenatal steroid use. Management includes oxygen, CPAP, ventilation, antibiotics and NGT. Newborns usually develop RDS within 3 days of birth.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Respiratory Distress Syndrome

text: Definition: cessation of breathing for longer than 20 seconds, possibly with bradycardia. May be due to prematurity or secondary to other conditions such as respiratory distress, infection, cold‑stress, low blood volume, hypoglycaemia, CNS disorders, perinatal compromise or prematurity.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Apnoea

text: Determine cause and treat. General measures: tactile stimulation, correct anaemia, maintain normal temperature, correct electrolyte imbalance, look for intraventricular haemorrhage, sepsis, PDA, necrotising enterocolitis, GER and treat accordingly. Give aminophylline for prevention of apnoea of prematurity (6 mg/kg PO loading dose then 2.5 mg/kg BD PO). Caffeine is preferred where available.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Apnoea – Treatment

text: Young infants with breathing difficulties present with fast breathing and feeding difficulties. On examination there may be grunting, head nodding, cyanosis, intercostal/sternal/subcostal recession; auscultation may reveal crepitations and/or wheezes.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Young Infant

text: If breathing < 60 bpm, attempt breastfeeding. If the baby is struggling, switch to oro‑ or nasogastric tube feeds. If breathing 60‑80 bpm, give oro‑/nasogastric feeds every two hours with expressed breast milk (restricted maintenance volumes). If breathing > 80 bpm, consider IV fluids 100 mL/kg/day.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Young Infant > Supportive Treatment – Nutrition

text: Ensure airway is clear. Start oxygen via nasal prongs/catheter 0.5 L/min. Re‑assess after 15‑30 min; if SpO₂ < 90 % increase to 1 L/min (max 2 L/min). Re‑assess after another 30 min; if still < 90 % give 4 L/min via face mask. Consider CPAP if no improvement and no contraindications.

text:
HIE is also called birth asphyxia and neonatal encephalopathy. It is very common in Malawi, making optimal management of these newborns essential.
Lack of oxygen supply to organs before, during, or immediately after birth results in HIE, recognised by delayed onset of breathing or crying. Clinical features that evolve over the first 2‑3 days of life include irritability, lethargy or coma, hypotonia or hypertonia, convulsions, apnoea, temperature instability, poor suck, and feeding difficulty. Diagnosis in pre‑term infants is difficult because prematurity itself can cause hypotonia.
Suspect HIE in a baby with some or all of the following:
- Foetal bradycardia
- Prolonged second stage of labour
- Required bag‑and‑mask ventilation for > 5 min
- Required cardiac massage
- Irritability, hypotonia, seizures, poor suck, poor colour
- High lactate on cord blood
- APGAR score < 7 at 5 min
The APGAR score is used to assess the newborn’s condition at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. A score < 7 at 5 minutes is a red flag for possible HIE.

Severity is classified into mild, moderate, and severe based on clinical features (consciousness, tone, seizures, and sucking/respiration). Babies with mild or moderate HIE generally have a good prognosis, whereas severe HIE is associated with poor outcomes.

text: Management focuses on supportive care, seizure control, maintenance of temperature, glucose, fluids, and feeding, and careful documentation and follow‑up.

Check for emergency signs (ABC) and provide emergency care.
Place the baby under a radiant warmer to maintain normal temperature while preventing hyperthermia.
Check blood glucose; treat hypoglycaemia if present, otherwise re‑check every 12 hours.
Provide maintenance intravenous fluids using wall charts after stabilising emergency signs.
Consider enteral feeding if no emergency signs or abdominal distension. Initiate breast feeding if sucking is good; otherwise, tube‑feed expressed breast milk using wall‑chart‑derived volumes.
section: Management of Birth Asphyxia / HIE > Key Facts for Providers – Supportive Management of Birth Asphyxia

text: Refer to the dedicated convulsion management protocol (link provided in the original document).
section: Management of Birth Asphyxia / HIE > Management of Convulsions

text:
Document the case in the health passport and classify HIE as mild, moderate, or severe.
If severe, arrange a physiotherapy appointment (initially floppy infants often become stiff, requiring the mother to learn massage and basic physiotherapy skills).
Schedule follow‑up at 6 months focusing on head growth, general health, and motor neuro‑development. Further assessments at 12‑24 months should concentrate on cognitive and language development.

In 2018 the WHO estimated that 10.5 % of babies born in Malawi are low‑birth‑weight, the majority being premature. A neonate < 2500 g is classified as LBW. Nearly 75 % of neonatal deaths occur among LBW neonates. Even after surviving the acute phase they remain at higher risk of malnutrition, recurrent infections and neuro‑developmental impairment. LBW may be caused by prematurity, intra‑uterine growth retardation (IUGR) or both; distinguishing the aetiology guides further management.

text: A healthy LBW newborn ≥ 1500 g can be cared for at home under supervision of a health‑care worker. Babies < 1500 g are at risk of hypothermia, feeding problems, apnoea, RDS and necrotising enterocolitis and usually need facility‑based care with close monitoring.

text: Continuous Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) = skin‑to‑skin contact with the mother, preferably with exclusive breast‑milk feeding. Intermittent KMC = skin‑to‑skin alternated with warming devices. Three categories of KMC in Malawi:

text: Place baby upright in mother's clothing, ensure baby’s head is turned to one side, tie securely, cover both with mother's clothes. Allow baby to breast‑feed at least every 2 h, keep mother propped up, add blankets if room is cold, enlist another caregiver for KMC when mother rests.

All babies who require administration of intravenous fluids, especially for maintenance or resuscitation will need a syringe driver to deliver the fluids.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text:
Patients who are not being fed are at risk of developing hypoglycaemia and dehydration. Syringe drivers are used for the controlled infusion of small volumes of intravenous fluids over minutes or hours.

A syringe driver uses an internal motor to push a syringe plunger at very slow rates to ensure controlled delivery of low volumes of fluid. Syringe drivers may be calibrated for specific syringe brands and sizes and may have internal medicine libraries that can store flow rates and delivery volumes for specific types of fluids.

Patients should receive intravenous fluid per hour, based on their weight and age. Due to their small weights, neonates should never be given fluid too quickly or in too large volumes.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text: When fluid delivery is complete and no further infusion is needed, press Stop on the syringe driver, remove the extension tubing and cap the cannula.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text: Errors related to syringe type may cause inaccurate fluid volumes because the driver may be calibrated for a specific brand or size of syringe.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text: Syringe drivers are usually mains‑powered with a rechargeable battery. Keep the device on its charger when not in use, but remove it only when needed to ensure a charged battery is available during power outages.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text: Drivers may be wall‑ or pole‑mounted for easy access. Multiple drivers can be used on a single patient; they must be secured while in use and syringes clearly labelled by content.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text: Set up the driver as if preparing for a patient and run it for 15 minutes each week if not in clinical use. Visually inspect for missing parts or alarm indicators during this test run.

text: Infants have a large volume of bilirubin due to high red‑cell mass and rapid breakdown. The immature liver often cannot clear bilirubin quickly, leading to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice). Phototherapy is indicated for visible jaundice at birth, jaundice extending below the umbilicus, bilirubin levels above treatment thresholds, and in the presence of prematurity, sepsis, bruising/cephalohaematoma, maternal‑infant blood incompatibility, or G6PD deficiency.

Phototherapy uses blue light (425‑475 nm) to convert unconjugated bilirubin into a water‑soluble form. Devices can be overhead, over‑/under‑body, or flexible blankets. LEDs are preferred over halogen/fluorescent bulbs because of efficiency, durability and lower heat output.

Phototherapy need can be determined by serum bilirubin, transcutaneous bilirubin, or visual assessment using Kramer’s scale. In the absence of serum values, treat any visible jaundice on day 1, or Kramer level 3 on day 2+ (jaundice visible below the umbilicus).
Reference table showing bilirubin thresholds for phototherapy and exchange transfusion for term and pre‑term infants across days of life.
section: Assessment > TABLE 2.1 JAUNDICE & PHOTOTHERAPY VS TRANSFUSION

text: Kramer’s scale is the only studied proxy when serum bilirubin is unavailable. It is most reliable for ruling out levels >12 mg/dL in term infants using zones 1‑2.
section: Assessment > ALERT 2.1 – Physical exam estimation of serum bilirubin

text: Guidelines for using an overhead phototherapy unit: set‑up, patient preparation, start‑up, ongoing care, and removal.
Collect device, power cable, and light meter. Verify blue light emission. Position meter on mattress. Adjust height (≈20‑30 cm) to achieve therapeutic irradiance (25‑35 µW/cm²).
section: Management > SETTING UP FOR A PATIENT

text: Explain procedure, perform hand hygiene, gather eye mask/gauze, remove clothing leaving only a minimal diaper, place eye protection securely.
Place infant directly under the switched‑on light, document start date/time.

Provide continuous therapy, monitor vitals, rotate skin every 4 h, check bilirubin (serum or Kramer), ensure hydration, weigh daily, verify eye‑mask integrity, assess for worsening jaundice and adjust irradiance if needed.
Discontinue phototherapy when Kramer zones are resolved or bilirubin <50 mmol/L (or 3 mg/dL below treatment threshold). Turn off light, remove eye protection, continue monitoring for 24‑48 h.
Routine cleaning and hand hygiene are essential to avoid healthcare‑associated infections with phototherapy equipment.
Hand hygiene before/after use, use new or cleaned eye coverings, store equipment dry, avoid cable kinks, single‑patient use per unit.
Turn off and unplug unit, disinfect handles and LCD with alcohol, clean housing per ward guidelines.
Both clinical and device‑related complications can occur with phototherapy.
Dehydration, hypothermia, retinal damage, eye infections, bronze‑baby syndrome, and acute bilirubin encephalopathy (kernicterus).
Loss of therapeutic output after 20 000‑50 000 h of use; regular irradiance testing required.
Basic first‑line care to prolong equipment life.
Units may be mains‑powered or battery‑backed depending on model.
Typically rolling units with caster wheels; can be moved bedside as needed.
Test light output weekly with a phototherapy light meter (see ‘Setting up for a patient’).
First‑line steps before contacting biomedical engineering.
Check power cable, switch and outlet (7.1). If still non‑functional, contact maintenance.

text: Contact maintenance for bulb replacement. Verify you have switched on the blue therapeutic lights, not just the white examination lights (7.2).

text:
Learning objectives:
Describe why a glucometer is essential for detecting and managing neonatal hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia.
Identify the clinical signs and risk‑factors that should prompt blood‑glucose testing in newborns.
Perform a complete glucometer‑based blood‑glucose assessment, from preparation through result documentation.
Apply infection‑prevention and device‑cleaning practices to minimise cross‑contamination.
Recognise common clinical and device‑related complications of glucometer use and know how to respond.
Execute routine care, preventive maintenance and basic troubleshooting of a glucometer.

Assessment of blood glucose with a glucometer should be performed on routine admission for all infants and continued for all sick or at‑risk newborns.  Hypoglycaemia may present with jitteriness, irritability, hypotonia, lethargy, reduced consciousness, poor feeding, seizures, hypothermia, apnoea/irregular breathing, or it may be asymptomatic.  Prematurity, intra‑uterine growth restriction, birth‑asphyxia, infants of diabetic mothers and other sick babies are especially prone to hypoglycaemia.

Hypoglycaemia occurs in ~10 % of healthy neonates and is the most common neonatal medical emergency.  Glucometers provide rapid, approximate whole‑blood glucose values; results should be confirmed by laboratory testing when persistent, recurrent, or accuracy is in doubt.  Test strips use a glucose‑oxidase electrode that generates a current proportional to glucose concentration.

Malawi is one of the countries that made significant progress in reducing child mortality, achieving MDG 4 targets ahead of the 2015 deadline recognised in the Promise Renewed report of 2014. The current under‑five mortality rate has fallen to 71 per 1,000 live births from 244 in 1999. Neonatal mortality is still very high at 27 per 1,000 live births and accounts for over 40 percent of under‑five mortality. Newborn survival is receiving international attention, with an established global target of 12 deaths per 1,000 live births by 2030. Newborns in Malawi continue to die from preventable conditions—infection, prematurity and asphyxia. Malawi’s Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP) targets reducing preventable neonatal deaths from 27 per 1,000 live births to 23 by 2020 and to 15 per 1,000 live births by 2035. Improving quality of care is one of the pillars for ensuring attainment of the objectives.

The Ministry of Health (MoH) fully recognizes the immediate and long‑term health, social and economic repercussions of acute and prolonged morbidity and mortality among neonates and infants. In the last few years there have been major advances for the care of newborns in Malawi. To achieve these ambitious targets purpose‑built special newborn care units (SNCU) have been opened in most hospitals. Nurses and midwives are now assigned to the neonatal wards and a package of equipment for comprehensive care has been provided to every hospital in Malawi. Trained and skilled health workers are one of the cornerstones of quality care and in order to address the knowledge and skills gap that were there, the MoH in collaboration with the Paediatric and Child Health Association (PACHA) and UNICEF developed the Care of the young Infant and Newborn (COIN) training course in 2015. The COIN manual and course have been updated on a regular basis to reflect new developments in neonatal care and respond to changes in practice.

The use of modern technologies played a vital role in improving outcomes of neonates in the West and is critical if Malawi is to achieve the ambitious neonatal mortality targets. To address this need the MOH partnered with NEST 360° (Rice University and Malawi College of Medicine) to create a package of durable, high‑impact, low‑cost technologies for comprehensive newborn care. For these reasons a supplement has been added to the COIN course to explain the use and maintenance of the technologies. The manual is intended to be used by clinicians, nurses/midwives and biomedical technicians/engineers involved in taking care of neonates and young infants with the equipment placed in the SNCUs. The manual contains guidelines on basic clinical management of major causes of morbidity and mortality of neonates and young infants presenting to health facilities in Malawi. It is complementary to the existing MoH policies and guidelines on Maternal, Newborn and Child Health.

It is the hope of the Ministry that this document will be widely disseminated and utilised across the country to achieve the desired goal. The MoH would like to acknowledge and appreciate contributions made by individuals and institutions in various forms.

Dr Charles Mwansambo
Secretary for Health

section: Foreword > Purpose & Audience of the Manual

text: The Ministry hopes for wide dissemination and thanks contributors.

Most deaths in neonates and young infants can be prevented with low‑cost interventions. It has been estimated that up to half of all preventable neonatal deaths could be reduced with optimal treatment of neonatal illness.
In addition to providing care to newborns at birth, a district health facility receives sick young infants with diverse clinical presentations, some of whom are extremely sick and need emergency treatments. This manual and the course associated with it will deal with care of newborns at birth, the first few days of life, and sick young infants who are likely to be encountered in a secondary‑level health facility.
There is a lot of overlap between the clinical presentation and the management of conditions in the neonate and the young infant (defined as an infant less than two months of age). In this manual, when referring to both age groups we will discuss as the Neonate/Newborn and Young Infant (NYI). The young infant who requires resuscitation may be a neonate who is only a few days old. The approach to initial resuscitation is slightly different between the neonate and young infant, but the skills required are the same and you will practice these during this course.
This manual is for the candidate participating in a training on the Care of the Young Infant and Newborn (COIN). Lectures, videos, drills, and scenarios are the main methods used to deliver the course. The training is targeted at nurses, midwives, clinical officers, medical officers and medical assistants. The course is evidence‑based and strives to balance best practice with pragmatic solutions for our setting. Clear directions are provided for common clinical situations; if unclear, learners are encouraged to consult a colleague or a central hospital.
In the special newborn care unit, it is usual to have medical equipment not designed for hot, humid, dusty environments with an unstable power supply. Clinicians are often untrained on the use of these technologies and unsure of how to clean them or who to contact should the equipment fail. In addition to clinical knowledge and skills, the course equips participants on the management and use of technologies used in special newborn care units.
At the back of the manual there are wall charts or job‑aids that can be printed and placed in all clinical areas (clinic, ward, and special newborn care unit) where NYI are cared for.
On the last day of the COIN course, there is an examination that includes a Multiple Choice Questions (MCQ) paper to test knowledge and a competency‑based assessment that tests skills. Attitude (attendance and participation) is assessed throughout the course.

The NYI is most vulnerable to hypothermia during the first few hours after birth, although the condition may occur later too, for example during bathing, on a cold night, or during transportation, if measures to keep the baby warm are inadequate. Sick or low birth weight babies admitted to neonatal units with hypothermia are more likely to die than those admitted with normal temperatures.

Axillary temperature: This method is the routine practice for measuring temperature. It has fewer risks than rectal measurements in terms of injury or infection. Point the tip of the thermometer to the shoulder not the bed to avoid the thermometer reading the air temperature. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.

Rectal temperature: Do not use this method for routine monitoring. However, it is the best guide for core temperature in cold (hypothermic) sick neonates. Lubricate the thermometer, insert the tip of the thermometer into the rectum not more than an inch. Record the temperature after the reading has stabilized with a bleep.
section: Introduction > Temperature Recording Using a Digital Thermometer

text: An easy way to assess a newborn baby’s temperature is by “touch”. This can be easily taught to caregivers. The baby’s abdomen is felt with the back of hand and compared with their own forehead. Abdominal temperature represents the core temperature and it is reliable in the diagnosis of hypothermia. Warm and pink feet of the baby indicate that the baby is in thermal comfort. But when feet are cold and trunk is warm, it indicates that the baby has cold stress.

The NYI has a normal body temperature between 36.5‑37.4 °C.

**Classification table**
- **Mild**: 36.0‑36.4 °C
- **Moderate**: 32.0‑35.9 °C
- **Severe**: < 32 °C

A community‑based study has found that mortality increased by approximately 80 % for every degree Celsius decrease in first observed axillary temperature, and that relative risk of death ranged from 2 to 30 times for moderate hypothermia, increasing with greater severity. Other epidemiological data from India, Malawi and additional studies are summarized in the source text.
Newborn baby’s temperature falls within seconds of being born and sick NYI are often hypothermic. If the temperature continues to fall the baby will become sick and may even die.
section: Classification of Hypothermia > Four ways a neonate may lose heat to the environment

text: These are procedures to be taken at birth and during the next few hours and days in order to minimize heat loss in all newborns.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn

text: If there are no signs of distress, a mother can provide a warm environment with skin‑to‑skin contact for the baby. If the baby is < 2500 g this should be continued as KMC. Place the baby, with a nappy and hat, upright inside mother’s clothing against mother’s bare skin over the chest. A loose blouse, sweater, or wrap tied at the waist holds the baby. The baby should wear a hat. Let baby suckle at the breast as often as needed, but at least every 2 hours.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Skin‑to‑skin Contact (Kangaroo Mother Care)

text: Bathing should be delayed until at least 24 hours after birth. Blood, meconium and some vernix will have been wiped off during drying at birth. The remaining vernix does not need to be removed; it may reduce heat loss and is re‑absorbed through the skin during the first days of life.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Postpone Bathing

text: Weighing the baby at birth puts him/her at risk of heat loss and should be postponed for at least two hours unless the room temperature is warm.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Postpone Weighing

text: As a rule, newborns need one or two more layers of clothing and bedding than adults. Covers should not be tight to allow air spaces between the layers as trapped air is a very efficient insulator. Keep ambient atmospheric temperature warm for the baby’s weight and post‑natal age. Monitor body temperature every 4 hours.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Appropriate Clothing and Bedding

text: If a baby cannot stay with their mother for KMC, a hot‑cot is helpful. The Blantyre hot‑cot is a simple incubator that uses four 60‑watt light bulbs to raise the air temperature within the cot by 1.5 °C per bulb. A baby may need one, two, three or all four bulbs on to stay warm. Check the baby’s temperature after an hour in the cot; if temperature increases, turn off two bulbs to avoid overheating and re‑check regularly.
section: The Warm Chain for the Newborn > Prevention of Hypothermia in the Neonate > Place in a hot‑cot (warmer) when mother cannot stay with the baby

text: The NYI should be re‑warmed carefully and gradually to prevent seizures; the temperature needs to rise by 0.5 °C per hour. The method selected depends on how sick the NYI is and on the availability of mother, staff and equipment.
section: Treatment of Established Hypothermia in the NYI

Radiant warmers may be used on any neonatal patient admitted to the nursery ward, but especially for:

- Initial assessment of a sick or premature baby
- Hypothermia
- Undertaking invasive procedures
- Resuscitation
- When stabilising a sick baby

See the dedicated Radiant Warmer module for more information.

Hypoglycaemia is common in low‑birth‑weight and very sick newborn infants (NYI) and should always be considered early in management. 20 % of infants below seven days of age have hypoglycaemia. It is important to identify hypoglycaemia because it is associated with convulsions, permanent brain injury and increased mortality.

The letters C in “ABCCCD” stand for Circulation, Coma and Convulsions. This module will help with the systematic assessment, resuscitation and treatment of all NYI with life‑threatening conditions that are most frequently seen in infants less than 2 months of age.

Shock, also called ‘severe impaired circulation’, can be defined as the life‑threatening failure of adequate oxygen delivery to the tissues and may be due to decreased blood perfusion of tissues, inadequate blood oxygen saturation, or increased oxygen demand from the tissues that results in decreased end‑organ oxygenation and dysfunction. If left untreated, shock results in sustained multiple organ dysfunction, end‑organ damage and possible death.

All sick infants are assessed for Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Coma, Convulsions and severe Dehydration (ABCCCD). Efforts should be made to maintain normal blood glucose and a normal body temperature while managing ABCCCD.

Rapid assessment of circulation after airway and breathing. Signs include: baby alert?, hands cold?, temperature gradient up limbs?, capillary refill time (CRT) > 3 s?, pulse fast and weak?. Shock is present when 3 or more signs are noted.
section: Assessment > Assess Circulation for Signs of Shock

text: Look for dehydration (sunken eyes/decreased skin turgor), pallor, severe wasting/oedema, and assess oxygen saturation, heart rate and blood pressure.

Capillary refill is assessed by applying finger pressure to the centre of the chest over the sternum for 3 seconds. The time from release of pressure to full return of pink colour should be < 3 seconds. Longer times suggest shock, unless the environment is cold.
A strong, non‑fast radial pulse (≤ 160 bpm) is adequate. Absence of a radial pulse or a weak/faster pulse requires checking the femoral pulse and assessing hydration status.
Treatment of shock requires teamwork. The following actions need to be started simultaneously, beginning with fluid administration for shock or impaired circulation.

NYI have immature immune systems and have just been colonised with bacteria during their recent delivery. They are therefore prone to infections which are likely to cross barriers (lungs‑blood, gut‑blood, meninges). Many NYI infections can be prevented by good hygiene at birth, early and exclusive breastfeeding, appropriate umbilical cord care, appropriate eye care, using Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) and avoiding separation of the mother and infant.

Common systemic bacterial infections in young infants include sepsis, pneumonia, urinary tract infection and meningitis and all these may present alike. Sepsis is a clinical syndrome of systemic illness accompanied by septicaemia (bacteria in the blood). It is also called bacteraemia.

**Risk Factors for Sepsis and Clues to Infection**

- Intrapartum maternal fever (temperature > 38 °C)
- Membranes ruptured > 18 h before delivery
- Foul‑smelling or purulent amniotic fluid
- Untreated STI such as syphilis
- Suspected or confirmed sepsis in a twin

Babies born to mothers with these risk factors may be symptomatic or asymptomatic. Both groups should be treated as having sepsis with IV antibiotics because the risk of neonatal infection and mortality rates are high.

ABCCCD – Assess ABCD and manage the problems identified. Provide supportive care and monitoring for the sick NYI.

Start empiric antibiotics; give penicillin and gentamicin – see doses in wall‑charts. Give (flu)cloxacillin instead of penicillin if extensive skin pustules or abscesses are present as these may indicate a staphylococcal infection.

It is a way of communicating with our colleagues, remembering important events and monitoring progress. Good documentation prevents errors and protects both the patient and the staff.
It is essential that the mother and baby are treated as one unit. The mother needs to know what is happening to her baby and how she can help. We must make her feel welcome, important and central to the well‑being of her baby.
The health workers are a team. We need to communicate with each other, respect and value each other and include the mother in the care of her baby. She is an extended part of the team.
The death of a baby is devastating to the mother and the baby’s family. We must be sensitive to their concern and grief, respectful of religious and cultural beliefs and be prepared to express our sorrow at their loss. Medical diagnoses and treatment given need to be explained to the family. It is important for the family to know that their loss is through no fault of the mother and she should not be blamed.
Poor communication leads to mistakes and to mismanagement. Poor clinical handover can result in:
section: Communicating in Health Care > Consequences of poor communication

text: Learn to listen; try to understand why and what is being asked. Is your body language welcoming and interested? Don’t stand over a person you are talking to.

Anticipating clinical deterioration and possibly stopping it from happening makes good sense. To do this monitoring is critical; it helps us decide when to act to prevent problems and when to call for help.
Nurses are with the patients all the time and measuring, monitoring, and assessing progress is one of their very important tasks.

Introduction of essential devices to newborn care units is critical to improving newborn survival.  Devices can increase hospital‑acquired infections if adequate disinfection and cleaning measures are not put into place.  This module focuses on infection‑control measures associated specifically with essential devices, staff and visitors.  For comprehensive guidance see local and WHO guidelines.

Hospitals, wards, equipment and staff are all sources of infection for a baby.  Hospital‑acquired infections (HAI) are often caused by multidrug‑resistant bacteria.  Prevention strategies include hand hygiene, environmental cleanliness, medical‑equipment maintenance and waste disposal.  Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) are key elements.

Hand hygiene is the single most important measure to reduce transmission of infections.  All HCWs, guardians and visitors should wash hands and forearms up to the elbow with soap and water on entry, dry with single‑use towels, and re‑wash whenever visibly soiled.  Alcohol‑based hand‑rub is acceptable when hands are not visibly dirty.  Hand hygiene should be the last action before patient contact and the first action after.

Gloves protect both HCWs and patients but do not replace hand hygiene.  Wear gloves only when necessary (e.g., contact with bodily fluids, invasive procedures, contaminated surfaces) and discard immediately after use.  Perform hand washing or sanitising promptly after glove removal.

Staff should wear clean clothing with bare arms below the elbow.  No jewellery (except plain wedding bands) and nails must be short, natural and unvarnished.  Gowns, when used, must not be shared or reused until re‑washed.
Any HCW or guardian with an acute or transmissible infection must not be on the ward.  Guardians with acute respiratory illness should wear a mask and practise meticulous hand washing.

Isolation policies should cohort at‑risk patients with similar infections.  Strict hand hygiene is required on entry and exit.  Equipment used in isolation areas must be cleaned and disinfected before returning to the main ward.

All neonatal equipment (suctions, CPAP, monitors, warmers, etc.) must be cleaned according to manufacturer instructions.  WHO recommends 0.5 % sodium hypochlorite for surfaces contaminated with blood/body fluids; 70 % alcohol for metal and rubber surfaces.

All horizontal surfaces (bed rails, tables, trolleys, commodes, taps, scales, etc.) must be cleaned and disinfected with an approved detergent or 0.5 % chlorine/70 % alcohol solution at least daily and whenever visibly soiled.  Between patient admissions, cots, incubators and patient beds must be cleaned thoroughly.  Floors, handles and other surfaces are cleaned daily per departmental policy.

Do NOT recap used needles, do NOT remove needles from syringes by hand, and do NOT bend or break used needles.  Place syringes, lancets, needles and other sharps promptly in puncture‑resistant containers.

All single‑use devices should be used once, but in resource‑limited settings they are often reused.  Departmental policy must be strictly followed; cleaning must occur promptly after use.  Respiratory circuits, tubing and equipment are commonly reprocessed, whereas syringes, needles and disposable gloves must never be reprocessed.

This is the critical period of transition from intra‑utero life to extra‑utero independent existence. Effective care at birth is needed to anticipate problems with this transition and to provide support to ensure stabilization. Most babies born with apnoea at birth will start to breathe themselves within 60–90 seconds if they have a clear airway.
section: Why is care at birth important?

text: Approximately 10 % of newborns require some assistance to begin breathing at birth; very few, only about 1 % need more than basic resuscitation to survive.

These are deliveries where it is more likely that resuscitation will be required. They include deliveries to mothers who are sick, who had a previous foetal or neonatal death, with pre‑eclampsia or eclampsia, with multiple pregnancies, or who are delivering preterm. Delivery circumstances that increase the risk are abnormal presentation, prolonged second stage, prolapsed cord, prolonged rupture of membranes, and meconium staining of the liquor.
For many infants, resuscitation cannot be anticipated before delivery. Therefore be prepared for resuscitation at **every** delivery.

The recommended temperature for the delivery room is 25 °C. Shut windows, turn off AC/fans, warm the resuscitaire 30 min before delivery and have at least two pre‑warmed absorbent towels – one to dry the baby and one to wrap the baby.
Nurse/midwife should identify a helper, explain roles and allocate duties according to skill level (qualified staff, untrained staff, or relative).
Check that all equipment and supplies are available, clean and functional before birth. Equipment should be positioned for immediate use.

Most newborns require only simple supportive care at and after delivery. Deliver the baby onto the mother’s abdomen, note the time of birth and wait one minute before clamping the pulsating cord. Use two dry warm cloths – one to dry and a fresh one to wrap. Immediate drying prevents heat loss and often stimulates breathing.
Assessment‑Decision table (crying → routine care; not crying but chest rising → routine care; gasping, not breathing, or very poor tone → start resuscitation immediately).
section: Baby is Delivered > How do you determine whether the baby requires resuscitation?

text: 1. Dry the infant with a clean cloth and observe. 2. Maintain skin‑to‑skin contact. 3. Cover to prevent heat loss. 4. Clamp and cut cord after ≥1 min. 5. Encourage breast‑feeding within the first hour. 6. Skin‑to‑skin & early breast‑feeding keep infant warm and prevent hypoglycaemia.
section: Baby is Delivered > Key Facts for Providers – How to Provide Essential Newborn Care at Delivery

text: If the baby requires resuscitation provide warmth with a radiant warmer or a warming cot. Position yourself at the bedside (head of the baby) so the chest and abdomen remain unobstructed.
A – Ensure Airway is open and clear. B – Ensure Breathing (spontaneous or assisted). C – Ensure adequate Circulation of oxygenated blood. Maintain body temperature throughout.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > ABCs of Resuscitation

text: Open airway by slightly extending the neck, maintain neutral position, use a rolled cloth if occiput is large. Clear secretions by wiping or suctioning (mouth‑first, then nose) with a Penguin suction device or catheter.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > A. Airway

text: Indications: baby not breathing or gasping, HR <100 bpm despite spontaneous breathing, persistent central cyanosis. Use appropriately sized masks (round or anatomically shaped). Position mask covering nose, mouth and chin; hold with thumb‑index‑middle fingers and lift the chin with ring/fifth fingers. Bag‑mask has a pop‑off valve set at ~30‑40 cm H₂O to prevent excessive pressure. Deliver 40‑60 breaths/min (≈1 breath/sec). For term infants use room air; for preterm ≤32 weeks start with 30 % O₂ (or air if blender unavailable). Assess effectiveness by chest rise, heart‑rate >100 bpm, improved colour and spontaneous effort.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > B. Positive‑Pressure Ventilation (Bag‑Mask)

text: Begin compressions if HR <60 bpm after 30 seconds of effective ventilation. Two providers are needed – one for ventilation (head of the baby) and one for compressions (side or foot). Techniques: Thumb technique (thumbs on sternum, fingers under back) or 2‑finger technique (middle finger + index/ring finger). Position hands on the lower third of the sternum (halfway between xiphoid and line between nipples). Compress to a depth of ~1⁄3 AP chest diameter, release fully. Ratio 3 compressions : 1 ventilation (3:1) ≈ 90 compressions/min. After ~30 seconds reassess HR. If HR >60 bpm stop compressions, continue ventilation at 40‑60 breaths/min. If HR remains <60 bpm continue CPR until HR >100 bpm or the baby is deemed non‑viable.
section: Steps to Follow During Resuscitation > C. Circulation – Chest Compressions

text: Discontinue efforts when: 1) infant is not breathing and no detectable heartbeat after 10 min; or 2) no spontaneous breathing and HR < 60 bpm after 20 min of effective resuscitation. Record the event, inform the parents and allow them to hold the infant if they wish.
A visual flow‑chart summarising the steps from initial assessment through airway, breathing, chest compressions and post‑resuscitation care.

Why are breathing difficulties in the newborn so important? Breathing difficulties are the most common way that sick neonates present to the healthcare worker. There are several different possible diagnoses and these need to be considered in order to provide the correct management.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)

text: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) occurs primarily in premature infants; its incidence is inversely related to gestational age and birthweight. It occurs in 60–80 % of infants < 28 weeks, 15–30 % of those 32‑36 weeks, about 5 % beyond 37 weeks and rarely at term. Surfactant deficiency is the primary cause. Risk is increased by maternal diabetes, multiple births, Caesarean section, precipitous delivery, asphyxia, cold stress and a history of previously affected infants; reduced by antenatal steroid use. Management includes oxygen, CPAP, ventilation, antibiotics and NGT. Newborns usually develop RDS within 3 days of birth.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Respiratory Distress Syndrome

text: Definition: cessation of breathing for longer than 20 seconds, possibly with bradycardia. May be due to prematurity or secondary to other conditions such as respiratory distress, infection, cold‑stress, low blood volume, hypoglycaemia, CNS disorders, perinatal compromise or prematurity.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Apnoea

text: Determine cause and treat. General measures: tactile stimulation, correct anaemia, maintain normal temperature, correct electrolyte imbalance, look for intraventricular haemorrhage, sepsis, PDA, necrotising enterocolitis, GER and treat accordingly. Give aminophylline for prevention of apnoea of prematurity (6 mg/kg PO loading dose then 2.5 mg/kg BD PO). Caffeine is preferred where available.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Newborn > Apnoea – Treatment

text: Young infants with breathing difficulties present with fast breathing and feeding difficulties. On examination there may be grunting, head nodding, cyanosis, intercostal/sternal/subcostal recession; auscultation may reveal crepitations and/or wheezes.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Young Infant

text: If breathing < 60 bpm, attempt breastfeeding. If the baby is struggling, switch to oro‑ or nasogastric tube feeds. If breathing 60‑80 bpm, give oro‑/nasogastric feeds every two hours with expressed breast milk (restricted maintenance volumes). If breathing > 80 bpm, consider IV fluids 100 mL/kg/day.
chapter: Breathing Difficulties in Newborns and Young Infants (Chapter 5)
section: Breathing Difficulties in the Young Infant > Supportive Treatment – Nutrition

text: Ensure airway is clear. Start oxygen via nasal prongs/catheter 0.5 L/min. Re‑assess after 15‑30 min; if SpO₂ < 90 % increase to 1 L/min (max 2 L/min). Re‑assess after another 30 min; if still < 90 % give 4 L/min via face mask. Consider CPAP if no improvement and no contraindications.

text:
HIE is also called birth asphyxia and neonatal encephalopathy. It is very common in Malawi, making optimal management of these newborns essential.
Lack of oxygen supply to organs before, during, or immediately after birth results in HIE, recognised by delayed onset of breathing or crying. Clinical features that evolve over the first 2‑3 days of life include irritability, lethargy or coma, hypotonia or hypertonia, convulsions, apnoea, temperature instability, poor suck, and feeding difficulty. Diagnosis in pre‑term infants is difficult because prematurity itself can cause hypotonia.
Suspect HIE in a baby with some or all of the following:
- Foetal bradycardia
- Prolonged second stage of labour
- Required bag‑and‑mask ventilation for > 5 min
- Required cardiac massage
- Irritability, hypotonia, seizures, poor suck, poor colour
- High lactate on cord blood
- APGAR score < 7 at 5 min
The APGAR score is used to assess the newborn’s condition at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. A score < 7 at 5 minutes is a red flag for possible HIE.

Severity is classified into mild, moderate, and severe based on clinical features (consciousness, tone, seizures, and sucking/respiration). Babies with mild or moderate HIE generally have a good prognosis, whereas severe HIE is associated with poor outcomes.

text: Management focuses on supportive care, seizure control, maintenance of temperature, glucose, fluids, and feeding, and careful documentation and follow‑up.

Check for emergency signs (ABC) and provide emergency care.
Place the baby under a radiant warmer to maintain normal temperature while preventing hyperthermia.
Check blood glucose; treat hypoglycaemia if present, otherwise re‑check every 12 hours.
Provide maintenance intravenous fluids using wall charts after stabilising emergency signs.
Consider enteral feeding if no emergency signs or abdominal distension. Initiate breast feeding if sucking is good; otherwise, tube‑feed expressed breast milk using wall‑chart‑derived volumes.

text:
Document the case in the health passport and classify HIE as mild, moderate, or severe.
If severe, arrange a physiotherapy appointment (initially floppy infants often become stiff, requiring the mother to learn massage and basic physiotherapy skills).
Schedule follow‑up at 6 months focusing on head growth, general health, and motor neuro‑development. Further assessments at 12‑24 months should concentrate on cognitive and language development.

In 2018 the WHO estimated that 10.5 % of babies born in Malawi are low‑birth‑weight, the majority being premature. A neonate < 2500 g is classified as LBW. Nearly 75 % of neonatal deaths occur among LBW neonates. Even after surviving the acute phase they remain at higher risk of malnutrition, recurrent infections and neuro‑developmental impairment. LBW may be caused by prematurity, intra‑uterine growth retardation (IUGR) or both; distinguishing the aetiology guides further management.

All babies who require administration of intravenous fluids, especially for maintenance or resuscitation will need a syringe driver to deliver the fluids.
chapter: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

text:
Patients who are not being fed are at risk of developing hypoglycaemia and dehydration. Syringe drivers are used for the controlled infusion of small volumes of intravenous fluids over minutes or hours.

A syringe driver uses an internal motor to push a syringe plunger at very slow rates to ensure controlled delivery of low volumes of fluid. Syringe drivers may be calibrated for specific syringe brands and sizes and may have internal medicine libraries that can store flow rates and delivery volumes for specific types of fluids.

Patients should receive intravenous fluid per hour, based on their weight and age. Due to their small weights, neonates should never be given fluid too quickly or in too large volumes.

text: Infants have a large volume of bilirubin due to high red‑cell mass and rapid breakdown. The immature liver often cannot clear bilirubin quickly, leading to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice). Phototherapy is indicated for visible jaundice at birth, jaundice extending below the umbilicus, bilirubin levels above treatment thresholds, and in the presence of prematurity, sepsis, bruising/cephalohaematoma, maternal‑infant blood incompatibility, or G6PD deficiency.

Phototherapy uses blue light (425‑475 nm) to convert unconjugated bilirubin into a water‑soluble form. Devices can be overhead, over‑/under‑body, or flexible blankets. LEDs are preferred over halogen/fluorescent bulbs because of efficiency, durability and lower heat output.

Phototherapy need can be determined by serum bilirubin, transcutaneous bilirubin, or visual assessment using Kramer’s scale. In the absence of serum values, treat any visible jaundice on day 1, or Kramer level 3 on day 2+ (jaundice visible below the umbilicus).

Provide continuous therapy, monitor vitals, rotate skin every 4 h, check bilirubin (serum or Kramer), ensure hydration, weigh daily, verify eye‑mask integrity, assess for worsening jaundice and adjust irradiance if needed.
Discontinue phototherapy when Kramer zones are resolved or bilirubin <50 mmol/L (or 3 mg/dL below treatment threshold). Turn off light, remove eye protection, continue monitoring for 24‑48 h.
Routine cleaning and hand hygiene are essential to avoid healthcare‑associated infections with phototherapy equipment.

Learning objectives:
Describe why a glucometer is essential for detecting and managing neonatal hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia.
Identify the clinical signs and risk‑factors that should prompt blood‑glucose testing in newborns.
Perform a complete glucometer‑based blood‑glucose assessment, from preparation through result documentation.
Apply infection‑prevention and device‑cleaning practices to minimise cross‑contamination.
Recognise common clinical and device‑related complications of glucometer use and know how to respond.
Execute routine care, preventive maintenance and basic troubleshooting of a glucometer.

Assessment of blood glucose with a glucometer should be performed on routine admission for all infants and continued for all sick or at‑risk newborns.  Hypoglycaemia may present with jitteriness, irritability, hypotonia, lethargy, reduced consciousness, poor feeding, seizures, hypothermia, apnoea/irregular breathing, or it may be asymptomatic.  Prematurity, intra‑uterine growth restriction, birth‑asphyxia, infants of diabetic mothers and other sick babies are especially prone to hypoglycaemia.

Hypoglycaemia occurs in ~10 % of healthy neonates and is the most common neonatal medical emergency.  Glucometers provide rapid, approximate whole‑blood glucose values; results should be confirmed by laboratory testing when persistent, recurrent, or accuracy is in doubt.  Test strips use a glucose‑oxidase electrode that generates a current proportional to glucose concentration.

Malawi is one of the countries that made significant progress in reducing child mortality, achieving MDG 4 targets ahead of the 2015 deadline recognised in the Promise Renewed report of 2014. The current under‑five mortality rate has fallen to 71 per 1,000 live births from 244 in 1999. Neonatal mortality is still very high at 27 per 1,000 live births and accounts for over 40 percent of under‑five mortality. Newborn survival is receiving international attention, with an established global target of 12 deaths per 1,000 live births by 2030. Newborns in Malawi continue to die from preventable conditions—infection, prematurity and asphyxia. Malawi’s Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP) targets reducing preventable neonatal deaths from 27 per 1,000 live births to 23 by 2020 and to 15 per 1,000 live births by 2035. Improving quality of care is one of the pillars for ensuring attainment of the objectives.

The Ministry of Health (MoH) fully recognizes the immediate and long‑term health, social and economic repercussions of acute and prolonged morbidity and mortality among neonates and infants. In the last few years there have been major advances for the care of newborns in Malawi. To achieve these ambitious targets purpose‑built special newborn care units (SNCU) have been opened in most hospitals. Nurses and midwives are now assigned to the neonatal wards and a package of equipment for comprehensive care has been provided to every hospital in Malawi. Trained and skilled health workers are one of the cornerstones of quality care and in order to address the knowledge and skills gap that were there, the MoH in collaboration with the Paediatric and Child Health Association (PACHA) and UNICEF developed the Care of the young Infant and Newborn (COIN) training course in 2015. The COIN manual and course have been updated on a regular basis to reflect new developments in neonatal care and respond to changes in practice.

The use of modern technologies played a vital role in improving outcomes of neonates in the West and is critical if Malawi is to achieve the ambitious neonatal mortality targets. To address this need the MOH partnered with NEST 360° (Rice University and Malawi College of Medicine) to create a package of durable, high‑impact, low‑cost technologies for comprehensive newborn care. For these reasons a supplement has been added to the COIN course to explain the use and maintenance of the technologies. The manual is intended to be used by clinicians, nurses/midwives and biomedical technicians/engineers involved in taking care of neonates and young infants with the equipment placed in the SNCUs. The manual contains guidelines on basic clinical management of major causes of morbidity and mortality of neonates and young infants presenting to health facilities in Malawi. It is complementary to the existing MoH policies and guidelines on Maternal, Newborn and Child Health.

It is the hope of the Ministry that this document will be widely disseminated and utilised across the country to achieve the desired goal. The MoH would like to acknowledge and appreciate contributions made by individuals and institutions in various forms.

Dr Charles Mwansambo
Secretary for Health

text: The Ministry hopes for wide dissemination and thanks contributors.

Most deaths in neonates and young infants can be prevented with low‑cost interventions. It has been estimated that up to half of all preventable neonatal deaths could be reduced with optimal treatment of neonatal illness.
In addition to providing care to newborns at birth, a district health facility receives sick young infants with diverse clinical presentations, some of whom are extremely sick and need emergency treatments. This manual and the course associated with it will deal with care of newborns at birth, the first few days of life, and sick young infants who are likely to be encountered in a secondary‑level health facility.
There is a lot of overlap between the clinical presentation and the management of conditions in the neonate and the young infant (defined as an infant less than two months of age). In this manual, when referring to both age groups we will discuss as the Neonate/Newborn and Young Infant (NYI). The young infant who requires resuscitation may be a neonate who is only a few days old. The approach to initial resuscitation is slightly different between the neonate and young infant, but the skills required are the same and you will practice these during this course.
This manual is for the candidate participating in a training on the Care of the Young Infant and Newborn (COIN). Lectures, videos, drills, and scenarios are the main methods used to deliver the course. The training is targeted at nurses, midwives, clinical officers, medical officers and medical assistants. The course is evidence‑based and strives to balance best practice with pragmatic solutions for our setting. Clear directions are provided for common clinical situations; if unclear, learners are encouraged to consult a colleague or a central hospital.
In the special newborn care unit, it is usual to have medical equipment not designed for hot, humid, dusty environments with an unstable power supply. Clinicians are often untrained on the use of these technologies and unsure of how to clean them or who to contact should the equipment fail. In addition to clinical knowledge and skills, the course equips participants on the management and use of technologies used in special newborn care units.
At the back of the manual there are wall charts or job‑aids that can be printed and placed in all clinical areas (clinic, ward, and special newborn care unit) where NYI are cared for.
On the last day of the COIN course, there is an examination that includes a Multiple Choice Questions (MCQ) paper to test knowledge and a competency‑based assessment that tests skills. Attitude (attendance and participation) is assessed throughout the course.
